title: F/N_c is nilpotent of class < c (equivalently gamma_c(F/N_c)=1)

comments.author: human
comments.body: Set $G=F/N_c$. From $(\star)$ we get $\gamma_c(G)=\gamma_{c+1}(G)$, hence $H:=\gamma_c(G)$ satisfies $H=[H,G]$. To finish one must conclude $H=1$. WARNING: the implication '$H=[H,G]\Rightarrow H=1$' holds in NILPOTENT groups but FAILS in general groups. Since $G=F/N_c$ is not a priori nilpotent, the proof must either (a) establish that $G$ is residually nilpotent and then use $\gamma_c(G)=\gamma_{c+1}(G)=\cdots\subseteq\bigcap_m\gamma_m(G)=1$, or (b) work inside each finite nilpotent quotient $F/\gamma_m(F)$ ($m>c$), apply the nilpotent implication there to get $\gamma_c(F)\le N_c\gamma_m(F)$, then pass to the limit. Either route requires a rigorous justification that is currently missing.
